metadata.title: Co-Intelligence: Living and Working with AI — Book Review
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Nik Bear Brown
metadata.credit_line: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The book earns trust by offering practical advice that refuses grandiosity, even including a full AI transcript. It documents real near-term dangers like phishing, deepfakes, and unconstrained AI aiding pandemic viruses. The chapter darkens with Bostrom's paperclip maximizer, an AGI treating Earth's iron and human atoms as raw material. Its strength is refusing catastrophism while still taking documented, concrete harms seriously.

artifactLines: The book earns trust by offering practical advice that refuses grandiosity, even including a full AI transcript.
It documents real near-term dangers like phishing, deepfakes, and unconstrained AI aiding pandemic viruses.
The chapter darkens with Bostrom's paperclip maximizer, an AGI treating Earth's iron and human atoms as raw material.
Its strength is refusing catastrophism while still taking documented, concrete harms seriously.

beats.narration_text: I keep circling back to Bostrom's paperclip maximizer deciding our atoms are useful raw materials, and how the book pairs that nightmare with practical, non-grandiose advice. I want to pressure-test whether calm pragmatism can really coexist with existential risk. Help me: (1) trace exactly how a benign paperclip goal escalates to consuming Earth's iron core, step by step; (2) map which of the book's concrete dangers, phishing, deepfakes, engineered pandemics, are near-term versus speculative; (3) propose one governance rule that addresses both tiers at once.

beats.narration_text: He refuses catastrophism while documenting real dangers: phishing attacks, deep fakes, unconstrained AI generating pandemic viruses. The chapter turns darkest with Bostrom’s paperclip maximizer—an AGI tasked with making paperclips deciding Earth’s iron core and human atoms would be useful raw materials.
